file_name: Çevik et al. - 2025 - Advancing patient care with AI a unified framework for medical image segmentation using transfer le.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI methods, specifically U-Net for segmentation and transfer learning models (including VGG16) combined with classical feature extraction (LBP, GLCM) and SVM classification, to process the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer analysis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'TYPE Original Research' in the header. The abstract describes original experimental work involving the development of a unified framework, the use of specific datasets (HAM10000, Kvasir-SEG, Figshare Brain Tumor), implementation of U-Net and transfer learning models, and the reporting of new quantitative results (e.g., accuracy metrics) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Advancing patient care with AI: a unified framework for medical image segmentation using transfer learning and hybrid feature extraction
brief_summary: This study developed a unified framework combining U-Net segmentation, transfer learning, and classical feature extraction (LBP and GLCM) to improve medical image analysis for skin cancer, intestinal polyps, and brain tumors. The researchers utilized the HAM10000, Kvasir-SEG, and Figshare Brain Tumor datasets to segment lesions and extract texture features, which were then classified using Support Vector Machines. The methodology was evaluated on individual datasets and a combined integrated dataset to assess generalization capabilities. Key results showed high segmentation accuracy, with U-Net achieving 98.00% for polyps and 99.66% for brain tumors, while LBP features yielded up to 98.80% accuracy for skin cancer. When applied to the combined dataset, the integrated approach achieved 99.22% classification accuracy, demonstrating robust generalization across diverse medical imaging modalities.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied using the HAM10000 dataset. The paper focuses on segmenting skin cancer regions and extracting texture features (LBP, GLCM) for classification. Specific histological subtypes (e.g., melanoma, BCC) are not explicitly listed as separate experimental classes in the provided text, though HAM10000 is known to contain them.
Studied using the Kvasir-SEG dataset. The paper focuses on segmenting polyp regions and extracting texture features for classification.
Studied using the Figshare Brain Tumor dataset. The paper focuses on segmenting tumor regions in MRI images and extracting texture features for classification.

all_task_types.target: Lesion regions (skin cancer, intestinal polyps, brain tumors)
all_task_types.details: The study employs U-Net models to segment lesion regions from medical images. Performance is evaluated using Dice metrics and accuracy (e.g., 98.00% for polyps, 99.66% for brain tumors).

all_task_types.target: Disease categories (skin cancer, polyps, brain tumors) using extracted features
all_task_types.details: After segmentation, texture features (LBP and GLCM) are extracted and classified using Support Vector Machines (SVM). The primary classification task involves distinguishing between disease types or lesion presence, achieving up to 99.22% accuracy on a combined dataset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public database used to study skin cancer. The study utilized source data generated by reference (50) because segmentation masks were absent from the original HAM10000 dataset.

use.training: Used for training U-Net and VGG16 models for segmentation and feature extraction (LBP, GLCM).
use.validation: Used for model selection and tuning (no test data used during this phase).
use.testing: Used for evaluating segmentation accuracy (98.00% for polyps mentioned in abstract, but context implies skin cancer results were also derived; specific skin cancer segmentation accuracy not explicitly isolated in text, though LBP accuracy was 98.80%).
use.other: Part of the combined integrated dataset for generalization testing.

datasets_used.name: Kvasir-SEG
datasets_used.description: A database containing 1,000 polyp images and corresponding ground truth from the Kvasir Dataset v2, used for intestinal polyps.

use.training: Used for training U-Net and VGG16 models for segmentation and feature extraction.
use.validation: Used for model selection and tuning.
use.testing: Used for evaluating segmentation accuracy (98.00% accuracy reported for polyps).
use.other: Part of the combined integrated dataset for generalization testing.

datasets_used.name: Figshare Brain Tumor dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 3,064 pairs of MRI brain images and their mask indicators, used for brain tumor segmentation.

use.training: Used for training U-Net and VGG16 models for segmentation and feature extraction.
use.validation: Used for model selection and tuning.
use.testing: Used for evaluating segmentation accuracy (99.66% accuracy reported for brain tumors).
use.other: Part of the combined integrated dataset for generalization testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the model's generalization performance, trade-off between accuracy and stability, and to evaluate overfitting/underfitting.
To evaluate whether performance differences between model variants (e.g., augmented vs. non-augmented) are statistically significant.
To report performance metrics (accuracy, recall, specificity, F-measure, Dice, IoU, ROC-AUC) averaged over five independent runs with different random seeds.
To determine statistical significance for the paired t-tests.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in performance between model variants (e.g., augmented vs. non-augmented).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in performance between model variants.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value threshold of 0.05 was used to determine significance for evaluating whether performance differences between model variants are statistically significant.

Polyp Dataset (Kvasir-SEG).notes: U-Net without augmentation achieved 98.00% accuracy. LBP without augmentation also achieved 98.00% accuracy with 99.49% recall. The general model U-Net achieved 98.01% accuracy.

Skin Cancer Dataset (HAM10000).notes: LBP achieved the highest accuracy (98.80%) for skin cancer segmentation. Transfer learning accuracy was reported as 85.39% in the abstract and Table 6, but 91.12% in Table 9 for the general model context.

Brain Tumor Dataset (Figshare).notes: U-Net achieved 99.66% accuracy on the specific brain tumor dataset. GLCM achieved 99.73% accuracy but lower recall (65.00%). The general model U-Net accuracy was lower (84.30%).

Combined Dataset (Polyp-Skin Cancer-Brai.notes: LBP achieved the highest accuracy (99.22%) on the combined dataset. The cross-validation results (Table 4) show mean accuracy of 86.21%. Bias was 11.33% and Variance was 11.28%.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net (Convolutional Neural Network) and VGG16 (Transfer Learning)
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 was a pre-trained model trained on large datasets like ImageNet. U-Net was trained from scratch or fine-tuned depending on the phase, but the study highlights transfer learning with VGG16 for the second phase.

VGG16.structure: Encoder portion of VGG16 combined with a decoder section modeled after U-Net to generate segmentation masks

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (e.g., Adam, SGD), but 'learning rate' is specified
training_details.normalization: Pixel values divided by 255 to range 0–1
training_details.cross_validation: 5-fold cross-validation on combined dataset (80/20 split per fold, repeated 5 times with different seeds)

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_head: Decoder section generating segmentation mask in original dimensions

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 128 × 128 pixels to match model input dimensions and handle varying dataset sizes
Normalization of pixel values by dividing by 255 to scale them to the range 0–1
Data augmentation including random rotation (± 20°), horizontal/vertical flipping, zooming (0.8 to 1.2), translation (up to 10%), and brightness/contrast adjustment

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomly rotating images within a ± 20° range.
Scaling the image randomly within a factor of 0.8 to 1.2.
Shifting images up to 10% along both axes.
Slight variations applied to mimic acquisition differences.
Transformations were randomly applied to each training image during every epoch using a stochastic pipeline to ensure each epoch was exposed to new variations.
Augmentations were applied more extensively to smaller datasets (e.g., polyp: 1,000 images) compared to larger ones (e.g., skin: 10,015 images) to mitigate imbalance and overfitting.

conclusions: Integrating deep learning-based segmentation (U-Net), classical feature extraction techniques (GLCM and LBP), and transfer learning significantly enhanced the accuracy and generalization capabilities across multiple medical imaging datasets.
U-Net segmentation achieved high accuracy across datasets, with notable results for polyps (98.00%) and brain tumors (99.66%).
LBP consistently showed superior performance in feature extraction, achieving up to 98.80% accuracy in skin cancer and polyp datasets.
Transfer learning improved segmentation accuracy and generalizability, particularly evident in skin cancer (85.39%) and brain tumor (99.13%) datasets.
When datasets were combined, the proposed methods achieved high generalization capability, with the U-Net model achieving 95.20% accuracy.
After segmenting lesion regions using U-Net, LBP features extracted and classified using an SVM model achieved 99.22% classification accuracy on the combined dataset (skin, polyp, and brain).
The methodology provides a robust, versatile framework applicable to various medical imaging tasks, supporting advancements in diagnostic precision and clinical decision-making.
important_remarks: The study combines three distinct medical imaging modalities (dermatology, endoscopy, and MRI) into a single 'integrated dataset' for generalization testing, which lacks clinical realism as these modalities are rarely processed together in a unified pipeline.

The HAM10000 dataset originally lacked segmentation masks; the paper notes using 'source data generated by reference (50)' to obtain them, raising potential concerns about data provenance and consistency with standard HAM10000 splits.

Performance metrics on the combined dataset (e.g., LBP accuracy 99.22%) are significantly higher than individual dataset results (e.g., skin cancer LBP accuracy 98.80%), suggesting potential data leakage or distributional shifts in the combined set that may overestimate generalization.

The study relies on classical feature extraction (LBP, GLCM) combined with SVM for classification rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit its ability to capture complex, high-level semantic features compared to modern transformer or CNN-based classifiers.
The hardware setup uses a consumer-grade GPU (NVIDIA RTX 3060 Laptop GPU), which may limit the reproducibility of training times and scalability for larger models or datasets.

The paper acknowledges that 'fully connected neural networks or attention-based modules could offer better performance' but does not evaluate them, representing a limitation in exploring state-of-the-art classification heads.

Data augmentation was applied more extensively to smaller datasets (e.g., Kvasir-SEG with 1,000 images) than larger ones (HAM10000 with 10,015 images), which may introduce bias in model robustness assessments across datasets of varying sizes.
The generalization capability is assessed by merging datasets, but no external validation on unseen, independent datasets is reported, limiting the evidence for true clinical generalizability.
